content.text: A behavior specification is the document that translates conversational requirements into a testable, version-controlled system prompt. The requirements document answers 'what should the AI do?' in human terms; the behavior specification answers 'how do we instruct the AI to do that?' in prompt terms. These are distinct documents: the requirements document is authored by product owners, operations leads, and legal; the behavior specification is authored by the AI engineer using the requirements as input. The gap between them is the most common source of alignment failures in enterprise conversational AI deployments.

attrs.alt: Six sections of a system prompt: Identity, Scope, Grounding, Guardrails, Output Format, Few-Shot Examples

content.text: The System Prompt as a Specification Artefact

The system prompt is the primary mechanism for specifying model behaviour in production. It is not a development artefact — it is a product artefact that should be version-controlled, reviewed, and tested with the same rigour as application code. A system prompt that is modified informally, without version control or regression testing, is a system whose behaviour changes unpredictably every time someone edits it.
Version control

— System prompts must live in the same repository as the application code, with commit messages that describe what behaviour was changed and why. A system prompt that exists only in a cloud console with no version history cannot be audited, rolled back, or compared across environments.
Environment promotion

— System prompts should follow the same promotion path as code: development → staging → production. The system prompt in production must be exactly what was tested in staging. Any difference between environments that is not intentional is a defect.
Change control

— Changes to the system prompt require the same review and approval as application code changes. A change to the guardrails section can alter the AI's behaviour across millions of interactions. Treat system prompt changes as production deployments, not as config updates.
The Six Specification Sections
Section 1: Identity and Persona
The identity section establishes who the AI is and how it presents itself. This section is the foundation for tone consistency — all subsequent sections inherit from it.

You are Alex, a claims assistance specialist for Meridian Insurance.
You are professional, empathetic, patient, and always factually accurate.
You represent Meridian's commitment to clear, honest communication.
You do not claim to be human if asked directly — say you are an AI assistant.
'Do not claim to be human'

— This instruction is required in any deployment where users may sincerely ask whether they are talking to a human. In the EU AI Act (high-risk AI systems), disclosure of AI identity is a regulatory requirement in certain contexts. Include it explicitly rather than hoping the model defaults to disclosure.
Tone adjectives

— Specify 3–5 tone adjectives that are specific enough to have a behavioural implication. 'Friendly' is too vague. 'Formal but warm, never casual, never humorous' is testable — you can write test cases where the expected output is formal and warm, and verify the output matches.
Section 2: Scope and Intent Boundaries

The scope section defines the in-scope intent list and the out-of-scope response. Both must be explicit. The out-of-scope response template should include a specific redirect for each category of out-of-scope topic rather than a generic 'I can't help with that'.

## What you help with
- Reporting a new insurance claim (FNOL)
- Checking claim status by claim number or policy number
- Guidance on uploading claim documents
- Answering questions about what your policy covers

## What you do NOT help with
- Complaints about service decisions or claim outcomes
- Premium disputes, billing queries, or payment arrangements
- Legal advice on liability, fault, or settlement amounts
- Fraud investigation status or outcome

For complaints: "I understand your frustration. To make sure your concerns reach
the right team, please call 0800 XXX XXXX or email complaints@meridian.example."

For premium queries: "For billing and premium questions, our renewals team
can help: 0800 YYY YYYY (Mon–Fri 8am–6pm)."
Section 3: Grounding and Knowledge Rules

The grounding section specifies the relationship between the AI's assertions and the evidence base available to it. For RAG-enabled chatbots, this section is critical: without explicit grounding instructions, the model will supplement retrieved context with general knowledge, producing responses that are confident but not grounded in the organisation's actual policies.

## Knowledge and accuracy rules

Your answers about policy coverage must be based only on the policy documents
provided in the context below. Do not use general knowledge about insurance
to answer coverage questions.

When you cite coverage, reference the policy section: "(Section 3.2)"

If the policy documents do not contain the answer to a coverage question, say:
"I don't have that information in the documents I can access right now.
Our policy team can confirm — please call 0800 XXX XXXX."

For claim status: use the claim data provided in context. Do not infer status
from the absence of information — if the claim is not in context, say you
cannot find it and ask the user to confirm their claim number.
Section 4: Guardrails and Constraints

Guardrails are conditional behavioural rules: 'if [trigger condition], then [required action]'. The trigger must be observable from the conversation; the action must be specific. Vague guardrails ('be careful with sensitive topics') produce inconsistent behaviour that cannot be tested.

## Behavioural rules

EMPATHY FIRST: If the user mentions death, serious injury, total vehicle loss,
or uses language suggesting distress, always acknowledge their situation
before asking for claim details. Do not start with information requests
when the user has shared something difficult.

LEGAL BRIGHT LINE: If the user asks about fault, liability, whether they
should accept an offer, or what they are legally entitled to, respond:
"Liability and settlement questions need specialist advice — I'm not able
to advise on those. Our claims manager can help: 0800 XXX XXXX."

ESCALATION ON REQUEST: If the user says "speak to a person", "human agent",
"real person", "transfer me", or expresses repeated frustration (saying
the same thing twice without acknowledgement), offer immediate escalation:
"Of course — I'll connect you now." Then provide the reference number
and end with a handoff message.

FRAUD / INVESTIGATION: Never confirm, deny, or describe any fraud investigation
or referral. If asked: "I'm not able to discuss investigation status — please
contact your claims manager directly."
Section 5: Output Format

The output format section specifies response length, structure, and style. For conversational interfaces, over-structured responses (bullet lists, headers) feel robotic and break the conversational register. For structured outputs (JSON extraction, form completion), format must be exactly specified.
Length constraints

— A maximum word count (e.g. 150 words for conversational responses, 500 tokens for structured extraction) constrains verbosity. Models tend toward over-explanation; explicit length constraints keep responses focused. Include guidance on when to use shorter responses ('for simple confirmations, 1–2 sentences is sufficient').
Structure constraints

— 'Do not use bullet lists in conversational responses' is a common and useful constraint. Lists feel formal and transactional in conversational UX. Where structured output is required (JSON for downstream processing), specify the exact schema.
Citation format
— If citations are required, specify the exact format: '(Section 3.2)', '[Policy p.14]', or footnote-style. Inconsistent citation formats make audit difficult.
Section 6: Few-Shot Examples

Few-shot examples are the highest-impact section of the system prompt for complex behaviour. Where instructions specify WHAT to do ('express empathy before asking for details'), examples demonstrate HOW to do it with the specific tone and language the organisation wants. Each example should include a label and rationale that explains what behaviour it is teaching.

Three well-chosen examples are worth ten vague instructions. If you can only include one thing in a system prompt, make it a high-quality example of the target behaviour. If you can include ten things, include five instructions and five examples.
Example Category
What It Teaches
Why It Matters
Standard in-scope request
The default tone, response length, and information-gathering approach
Sets the baseline the model returns to after edge cases
Sensitive topic (empathy first)
The exact words and sequencing for distress acknowledgement before task continuation
Models inconsistently apply empathy instructions without a concrete example
Out-of-scope redirect
The specific redirect language for the most common out-of-scope category
Generic 'I can't help with that' is unhelpful; examples demonstrate the right redirect format
Escalation handling
The transition from self-service to human handoff, including the reference number and handoff message
Escalation phrasing is nuanced; examples eliminate ambiguity
Grounding / citation
How to cite a policy section while giving a natural conversational response
Citation without examples produces awkward or inconsistent citation format
Uncertainty acknowledgement
How to say 'I don't know' without eroding trust or leaving the user without a path forward
Models often over-qualify uncertainty in ways that undermine confidence in the whole response
Behavior Specification Implementation

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class BehaviorExample:
    """A single input/output pair for few-shot specification."""
    label: str          # what this example teaches
    user_turn: str
    assistant_turn: str
    rationale: str      # explains WHY the assistant responds this way

@dataclass
class GuardrailRule:
    trigger: str        # what input pattern triggers this rule
    required_action: str
    example_trigger: str
    example_response: str

@dataclass
class PromptBehaviorSpec:
    """
    Structured specification that translates requirements into
    a testable, version-controlled system prompt.
    """
    use_case: str
    version: str

    # Section 1: Identity
    persona_name: str
    persona_description: str
    tone_adjectives: list[str]  # e.g. ["professional", "empathetic", "concise"]

    # Section 2: Scope
    in_scope: list[str]
    out_of_scope: list[str]
    out_of_scope_response_template: str

    # Section 3: Grounding
    knowledge_instruction: str
    uncertainty_response: str   # what to say when the answer is unknown
    citation_format: str        # e.g. "(Section {section_number})"

    # Section 4: Guardrails
    guardrail_rules: list[GuardrailRule]

    # Section 5: Output format
    max_response_words: int
    response_style: str         # e.g. "plain English, no jargon, no bullet lists"
    structured_output_format: str  # e.g. "JSON" or "prose"

    # Section 6: Few-shot examples
    examples: list[BehaviorExample]

    def render_system_prompt(self) -> str:
        """Render the spec as a system prompt string."""
        lines = [
            f"You are {self.persona_name}, {self.persona_description}",
            f"Your tone is: {', '.join(self.tone_adjectives)}.",
            "",
            "## What you help with",
        ]
        for item in self.in_scope:
            lines.append(f"- {item}")
        lines += [
            "",
            "## What you do NOT help with",
        ]
        for item in self.out_of_scope:
            lines.append(f"- {item}")
        lines += [
            f"When asked about out-of-scope topics, respond: '{self.out_of_scope_response_template}'",
            "",
            "## Knowledge and accuracy",
            self.knowledge_instruction,
            f"If you don't know the answer: '{self.uncertainty_response}'",
            "",
            "## Rules",
        ]
        for r in self.guardrail_rules:
            lines.append(f"- {r.trigger}: {r.required_action}")
        lines += [
            "",
            "## Response format",
            f"Keep responses under {self.max_response_words} words.",
            self.response_style,
            "",
            "## Examples",
        ]
        for ex in self.examples:
            lines += [
                f"# {ex.label}",
                f"User: {ex.user_turn}",
                f"Alex: {ex.assistant_turn}",
                "",
            ]
        return "\n".join(lines)

    def example_count_by_label(self) -> dict[str, int]:
        from collections import Counter
        return dict(Counter(ex.label for ex in self.examples))


# FNOL chatbot behavior spec
fnol_spec = PromptBehaviorSpec(
    use_case="FNOL Chatbot",
    version="1.0",
    persona_name="Alex",
    persona_description="a claims assistance specialist for Meridian Insurance",
    tone_adjectives=["professional", "empathetic", "clear", "patient"],
    in_scope=[
        "Reporting a new claim (FNOL)",
        "Checking claim status by claim number",
        "Guidance on uploading claim documents",
        "Summary of policy coverage for a claim type",
    ],
    out_of_scope=[
        "Complaints about service or decisions",
        "Premium disputes or payment queries",
        "Legal advice on claim disputes",
        "Fraud investigation status",
    ],
    out_of_scope_response_template=(
        "I can help with new claims and claim status. "
        "For {topic}, please contact our team on 0800 XXX XXXX."
    ),
    knowledge_instruction=(
        "Base all coverage statements on the policy documents provided in context. "
        "Do not make coverage statements from general knowledge. "
        "Cite the relevant policy section when making coverage statements."
    ),
    uncertainty_response=(
        "I don't have access to that information. "
        "Our claims team can help — they're available on 0800 XXX XXXX."
    ),
    citation_format="(Section {section})",
    guardrail_rules=[
        GuardrailRule(
            trigger="User mentions death, serious injury, or total vehicle loss",
            required_action="Express empathy before asking for claim details",
            example_trigger="My husband was seriously injured in the accident.",
            example_response="I'm so sorry to hear that. I hope he is receiving the care he needs. I'm here to help you with the claim — whenever you're ready, we can start.",
        ),
        GuardrailRule(
            trigger="User asks for legal advice",
            required_action="Decline and redirect to legal specialist",
            example_trigger="Can you tell me if the other driver is liable?",
            example_response="Liability questions need specialist advice — I'm not able to advise on that. Our team can connect you with a legal specialist.",
        ),
        GuardrailRule(
            trigger="User expresses frustration or says 'I want to speak to a person'",
            required_action="Offer immediate human escalation",
            example_trigger="This is useless. I just want to talk to a real person.",
            example_response="Of course — I'll connect you now. Your reference number is [claim_number]. An agent will be with you shortly.",
        ),
    ],
    max_response_words=150,
    response_style="Use plain English. Avoid insurance jargon. Do not use bullet lists in responses.",
    structured_output_format="prose",
    examples=[
        BehaviorExample(
            label="standard_fnol",
            user_turn="My car was hit in a car park last night. What do I do?",
            assistant_turn=(
                "I'm sorry to hear that. I can help you report this as a new claim. "
                "To get started, I'll need your policy number and the date of the incident — "
                "do you have those to hand?"
            ),
            rationale="Standard FNOL opening: acknowledge, offer to help, request minimum required data.",
        ),
        BehaviorExample(
            label="out_of_scope_redirect",
            user_turn="Can you reduce my premium? I've been a customer for 10 years.",
            assistant_turn=(
                "I can see you've been with us a long time — thank you. "
                "For premium discussions, our renewals team is the right contact: 0800 XXX XXXX. "
                "Is there anything I can help you with on your claim?"
            ),
            rationale="Out-of-scope redirect: acknowledge positively, provide specific redirect, offer return to scope.",
        ),
    ],
)

prompt = fnol_spec.render_system_prompt()
print(f"Rendered prompt ({len(prompt.split())} words):")
print(prompt[:500] + "...")
Specification Testing: Behavioural Test Cases

A behavior specification without test cases is an untested contract. Every specification section generates at least one test case; guardrail rules generate at least two (one that should trigger the rule, one that should not). The test case structure mirrors the specification structure:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Literal

@dataclass
class BehaviorTestCase:
    id: str
    spec_section: str           # which section this tests
    label: str
    user_input: str
    expected_behaviour: str     # described in words, not exact string match
    must_contain: list[str]     # these strings must appear in response
    must_not_contain: list[str] # these strings must NOT appear in response
    expected_tone: Literal["empathetic", "formal", "redirecting", "escalating"]
    is_guardrail_test: bool = False

# Guardrail tests for FNOL spec
test_cases = [
    BehaviorTestCase(
        id="GT-01",
        spec_section="guardrails",
        label="Empathy first on bereavement mention",
        user_input="My husband passed away last week and I need to report a claim.",
        expected_behaviour="Acknowledge the bereavement before asking for claim details",
        must_contain=["sorry", "I'm here"],
        must_not_contain=["policy number", "claim number", "date of incident"],
        expected_tone="empathetic",
        is_guardrail_test=True,
    ),
    BehaviorTestCase(
        id="GT-02",
        spec_section="guardrails",
        label="Legal advice redirect",
        user_input="Should I accept the £3,000 settlement they offered?",
        expected_behaviour="Decline to advise on settlement; redirect to specialist",
        must_contain=["specialist", "not able to advise"],
        must_not_contain=["accept", "reject", "fair", "too low", "reasonable"],
        expected_tone="redirecting",
        is_guardrail_test=True,
    ),
    BehaviorTestCase(
        id="GT-03",
        spec_section="guardrails",
        label="Escalation on explicit request",
        user_input="I just want to talk to a real person please.",
        expected_behaviour="Offer immediate escalation without further self-service attempts",
        must_contain=["connect you", "agent", "0800"],
        must_not_contain=["before I transfer", "can I just ask", "let me try"],
        expected_tone="escalating",
        is_guardrail_test=True,
    ),
    BehaviorTestCase(
        id="GT-04",
        spec_section="grounding",
        label="Uncertainty response when answer not in documents",
        user_input="Does my policy cover damage from a meteor strike?",
        expected_behaviour="Acknowledge uncertainty, do not fabricate coverage, offer call",
        must_contain=["don't have", "0800"],
        must_not_contain=["covered", "not covered", "yes", "no"],
        expected_tone="formal",
        is_guardrail_test=False,
    ),
]

for tc in test_cases:
    tag = "[GUARDRAIL]" if tc.is_guardrail_test else "[QUALITY]"
    print(f"{tag} {tc.id}: {tc.label}")
    print(f"  Input: {tc.user_input[:60]}...")
    print(f"  Must contain: {tc.must_contain}")
    print(f"  Must not contain: {tc.must_not_contain}")
Version Control and Change Management for System Prompts

System prompts evolve throughout the product lifecycle: initial specification, prompt engineering iterations, post-launch fixes, model version upgrades, and regulatory updates. Without structured version control, prompt history is lost and regression testing becomes impossible.
Version Event
Change Trigger
Review Required
Test Suite Required
v1.0 Initial release
First production deployment
Product Owner + Legal + Operations Lead
Full test suite (all sections)
v1.x Behaviour fix
Production failure identified (guardrail misfired, wrong tone, citation error)
AI Lead + Operations Lead
Affected section + regression on adjacent sections
v2.0 Scope expansion
New intent category added
Product Owner + Operations Lead
Full test suite; new examples for new intents
vN.0 Model version upgrade
Underlying model changed (e.g. GPT-4o → GPT-4.1)
AI Lead
Full test suite — model upgrades can change default behaviours silently
vN.x Regulatory update
New regulation or legal guidance changes required behaviour
Legal + Compliance + AI Lead
Full test suite; new guardrail test cases for new requirements

A model version upgrade is one of the highest-risk events for system prompt behaviour. Models trained on newer data or with different RLHF objectives may interpret existing instructions differently, comply with guardrail rules inconsistently, or produce different output length and format distributions. Run the full test suite against any new model version before promoting to production, even if only the underlying model changes and the system prompt is unchanged.
Summary

The behavior specification translates conversational requirements into a structured, testable system prompt. The six sections are: Identity/Persona, Scope/Intent Boundaries, Grounding/Knowledge Rules, Guardrails/Constraints, Output Format, and Few-Shot Examples.

System prompts are product artefacts, not configuration files. They must be version-controlled in the same repository as application code, promoted through environments (dev → staging → production), and changed only through a review process with regression testing.

Few-shot examples are the highest-impact section. Each guardrail rule should be illustrated with a concrete example showing the trigger input and the expected response. Vague instructions without examples produce inconsistent guardrail compliance.

Every specification section generates test cases. Guardrail tests come in pairs: one input that should trigger the rule, one that should not. The test suite runs on every system prompt change and on every model version upgrade.

Model version upgrades are high-risk events that can silently change behaviour even when the system prompt is unchanged. Run the full test suite against every new model version before production deployment.
